title: What each eval layer actually sees
subtitle: Three real failures that a vibe check and a string/citation eval wave through, and that this harness catches. Every number is a real eval score: the trajectory scorer, the held-out judge, and a cross-model run.

rows.scenario: Baseline cites a policy that doesn't exist
rows.detail: Cold-open Q89/Q102 - phantom "Vendor-Mgmt v2", "PCI-DSS certified". Citations are well-formed; the claim is false.

cells.note: verify-before-cite trajectory fails - the agent never called policy_exists_check

rows.scenario: Tuned prompt evaluated on an unseen framework
rows.detail: The citation checks still hold on ISO 27001, but the semantic judge does not: judge-accept drops from 0.85 on SOC 2 to 0.68 on the held-out set, and pass rate falls 14/20 to 7/20.

cells.label: judge-accept 0.85 -> 0.68
cells.note: held-out judge drop surfaces the overfit before it ships

rows.detail: gemini-2.5-flash to gemini-2.0-flash on the held-out set. Answers read fine; only the trajectory axis moves.

cells.label: answers read fine
cells.note: the final strings are well-formed on both models

cells.label: exec 1.00 -> 0.983
cells.note: one question slips the verify-before-cite ordering on the new model

footnote: The harness column is where mint fires: each cell is the harness catching a failure the naive layers waved through. Floor-raising, not benchmark-maxxing.
